query: high dose dexamethasone vs racemic epinephrine for treating croup

retrieved_docs.title: Laryngotracheobronchitis -- Treatment / Management -- Nebulized Epinephrine
retrieved_docs.content: Racemic epinephrine 2.25% 0.05 mL/kg nebulized with a maximum of 5 mL or L-epinephrine 0.5 mL/kg nebulized with a maximum of 5 mL is reserved for moderate and severe croup. Admit patients requiring more than 1 to 2 doses of nebulized epinephrine in the emergency department.

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: According to the Westley score, patients with severe croup or those showing persistent symptoms after 2 or more doses of racemic epinephrine should be considered for extended observation or hospital admission. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Croup is a common respiratory illness that affects the trachea, larynx, and bronchi, leading to inspiratory stridor and barking cough. This infection is a self-limited disease typically observed in children younger than 5. The clinical spectrum includes laryngotracheitis, laryngotracheobronchitis, and laryngotracheobronchopneumonitis. Most children with croup improve with a single dose of dexamethasone and supportive management. However, a few with severe symptoms require racemic epinephrine in addition to dexamethasone. Uncommon complications include bacterial tracheitis, pneumonia, pulmonary edema, and, rarely, death. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of croup, emphasizing the importance of the interprofessional team in improving care for patients with this condition. As an upper respiratory condition, stridor is the presenting symptom, which must be differentiated from wheezing caused by lower respiratory conditions. This activity emphasizes the importance of distinguishing between upper and lower respiratory signs and symptoms, as this differentiation guides appropriate management. The activity also discusses the differential diagnosis of croup and its management, highlighting the need for prompt differentiation of conditions, especially severe and rapidly deteriorating conditions such as epiglottitis, which require intensive care admission and actions to secure the airway.

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with moderate croup should be evaluated and monitored. These patients often require the administration of racemic epinephrine in addition to dexamethasone and supportive care. Patients can be discharged if  the following criteria are met: They are improved 3 hours after the last nebulized racemic epinephrine treatment Pulse oximetry reading is normal They tolerate oral fluids They have a non-toxic appearance, normal color, and no stridor at rest They have reliable caretakers who understand symptoms requiring a return visit They can return for close follow-up if needed

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Treatment / Management -- Epinephrine
retrieved_docs.content: Nebulized racemic epinephrine has been shown to improve symptom scores within 30 minutes, but its effects may diminish after 2 hours in patients with moderate-to-severe croup, who, therefore, require a prolonged period of observation after receiving racemic epinephrine. If symptoms improve, they can be discharged home after 4 hours of observation, with instructions for close follow-up. Administering approximately 0.5 mL/kg of L-epinephrine 1:1000 using a nebulizer appears more effective than racemic epinephrine at 2 hours due to its longer duration of action. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Treatment / Management -- Steroids
retrieved_docs.content: Dexamethasone is superior to budesonide for improving symptom scores, but does not appear to reduce readmission rates.

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Treatment / Management -- Steroids
retrieved_docs.content: Dexamethasone, a glucocorticosteroid, helps accelerate recovery, lowers the need for additional medical care, and shortens the length of hospital stay.

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Treatment / Management -- Steroids
retrieved_docs.content: Dexamethasone doses of 0.15, 0.3, or 0.6 mg/kg appear equally effective; 0.6 mg/kg is the most commonly used dose. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Laryngotracheobronchitis -- Treatment / Management -- Observation
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with croup should be observed for a minimum of 3 hours after the completion of each dose of nebulized racemic epinephrine. Watch for worsening or recurrence of symptoms, including persistent stridor at rest, increased work of breathing, and hypoxia, during the observation period. Admit patients requiring more than one or two doses of nebulized epinephrine in the emergency department.

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Treatment / Management -- Steroids
retrieved_docs.content: Oral dexamethasone is the preferred treatment for children with croup because it is effective, widely available, safe, and easily administered. A single dose is sufficient for most children. If symptoms persist for 3 to 4 days after the first dose of dexamethasone, a second dose can be administered.

retrieved_docs.title: Parainfluenza Virus -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment of croup: Corticosteroids have been shown to be beneficial for mild and moderate to severe croup. [1] Corticosteroids may be administered orally or given intramuscularly, and both have been shown to be superior to inhaled therapy. [1] The dosing is a single dose of dexamethasone at 0.6 mg/kg, although lower doses have been proposed. Epinephrine can be used nebulized, which results in some relief of symptoms within 30 minutes, but treatment effects do not last for more than 2 hours. This treatment may be beneficial and cause some relief while waiting for the anti-inflammatory activity of steroid therapy to take effect. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Based on the Westley croup score, treatment depends on the severity of signs and symptoms. Children with mild croup receive a single dose of dexamethasone, symptomatic treatment, and supportive care. Children with moderate-to-severe croup are given nebulized epinephrine and dexamethasone. [14] All patients with diminished oxygen saturation should receive supplemental oxygen. Moderate-to-severe cases require observation and admission if the symptoms do not improve. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Treatment / Management -- Steroids
retrieved_docs.content: The oral route is less invasive than an injection, making it more comfortable for children already experiencing respiratory distress. However, in cases where the child is unable to swallow, is vomiting, or has severe respiratory distress, intramuscular or intravenous dexamethasone may be necessary. Oral medication is also less costly and may be prescribed for home use in cases of mild croup.

retrieved_docs.title: Laryngotracheobronchitis -- Treatment / Management -- Corticosteroids
retrieved_docs.content: Regardless of severity, all patients with croup benefit from oral steroids. Dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg to 0.6 mg/kg by mouth with a maximum of 10 mg as a one-time dose is the typical dose (the intravenous dose can be given by mouth).

retrieved_docs.title: Croup -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with croup are often assessed by primary care clinicians or in emergency departments. Educating healthcare providers is essential to ensure they understand that croup is generally a self-limiting condition, with most patients achieving full recovery through supportive care alone and having an excellent prognosis. Many may benefit from pharmacological therapy in the form of a single dose of dexamethasone. A community-based study of children with croup found no difference in symptom scores between 3 daily doses of prednisolone (2 mg/kg) and 1 dose of dexamethasone (0.6 mg/kg). [29] In another study on pediatric patients with croup, steroids effectively reduced croup symptoms, shortened hospital admission stays, and reduced the need for follow-up visits. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Supraglottic Airway Obstruction -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids have proven to be of benefit for causes related to edema and inflammation of the upper airway in conditions such as croup. The mechanism of action includes anti-inflammatory and reduction of edema. Racemic epinephrine via nebulized route has both alpha and beta action and causes upper and lower airway lumen dilation, which improves air entry and results in a more laminar airflow.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1965
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroid dermatological preparations, plain encompass a range of topical corticosteroid strengths that are classified by potency groups. Within this category, “Corticosteroids, weak (group I)” represent the low‑potency end of the spectrum, while “Corticosteroids, very potent (group IV)” represent the high‑potency end. This hierarchical classification allows clinicians to select an appropriate plain corticosteroid preparation based on the severity of the skin condition, aiming to achieve therapeutic effectiveness while minimizing the risk of adverse effects associated with higher‑potency steroids.

Plain dermatological corticosteroid preparations constitute a subset of dermatological products and can be formulated across the full potency spectrum—from weak (group I) through moderately potent (group II) to very potent (group IV)—allowing therapy to be tailored to skin disease severity. The taxonomy further defines “corticosteroids, weak/moderately potent/potent, other combinations” as subtypes of the general “corticosteroids, other combinations” class. Hydrocortisone sodium succinate is noted as a therapeutic option for patients in a vegetative state, reflecting its role in critical care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1246
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids, weak (group I), Corticosteroids, potent (group III), and Corticosteroids, very potent (group IV) are all classified as plain corticosteroid dermatological preparations. These three potency groups represent increasing levels of anti‑inflammatory strength, allowing clinicians to select the appropriate topical corticosteroid formulation based on the severity of the skin condition and the desired therapeutic effect.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1846
retrieved_docs.content: Systemic glucocorticoids constitute a class of corticosteroid drugs that exert broad anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive actions throughout the body. Both prednison and rimexolone are individual agents that belong to this systemic glucocorticoid class, sharing the common pharmacologic properties of glucocorticoid receptor activation, suppression of cytokine production, and modulation of immune cell function. Recognizing prednison and rimexolone as members of the systemic glucocorticoid group helps clinicians understand their therapeutic indications, dosing considerations, and potential side‑effect profiles, which are characteristic of systemic glucocorticoid therapy.
